text: Salt-exposed plants experience many physiological and developmental changes due to regulation of the sodium ion (Na⁺) in order to deal with osmotic demands as well as ion toxicity. There is a pressing need to understand the integration and regulation of Na⁺ transport within and between cells as well as between different plant organs. Understanding where Na⁺ entry occurs, particularly the molecular basis involved in non-selective cation channel permeability and the roles of the HKT transporters, is important as is the role of Na⁺ efflux and the resultant vacuolar storage. Current understanding of xylem loading, the function of the HKT1;1-type salt-retaking transporters, the role of phloem Na⁺ re-translocation as well as salt glands and leaves will also be discussed. Understanding at what level environmental salt signal is processed into corresponding physiological plant responses is also required. It is an area that has seen tremendous advancement in recent years including roles for calcium, cyclic nucleotides (cGMP), reactive oxygen species and a multitude of hormones associated with salt-stress acclimation and signaling. Summarising and bringing together some of the latest information associated with early salt-signalling in plant cells that contribute to gene expression that may influence salt- tolerance, this review discusses the potential of a systems approach for designing improved salt tolerance in agronomic crop species through the manipulation of regulatory pathways.
